# Questions in Livestream 245

Live Stream #245: Friday The 23rd

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=680" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:20</a> | Does overnight shipping exist in Canada? | Yes, he says Amazon offers overnight shipping in Canada, but it is usually expensive; free shipping tends to take several days. | He answers simply that overnight shipping does exist in Canada and treats Amazon as the obvious example because it has tried to make that service available broadly. The catch is cost: in his experience overnight service is expensive, while free shipping normally means waiting around six or seven days. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=888" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:48</a> | If Ramesses later called for kidnapped Nubian labor, was that because the Hebrews had already left? | He says corvee labor from Nubia and Canaan was a normal New Kingdom practice and not necessarily a response to replacing Hebrews. | He does not know the exact reference but says New Kingdom kings, including Ramesses, regularly drew corvee or slave labor from vassal regions such as Nubia and Canaan. He adds that if the reference relates to later work at Abu Simbel, Nubian labor would make geographic sense because Abu Simbel was near Nubia, so the evidence does not require a replacement-for-Hebrews explanation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=1072" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:52</a> | How should one answer claims that Lorenzo Nigro has not shown the Jericho outer mudbrick wall is Late Bronze rather than Middle Bronze III? | He dismisses that pushback strongly and says the style of massive mudbrick fortifications fits Middle Bronze conventions, not the proposed revision. | He argues that Middle Bronze fortifications are typically massive cyclopean-style brick works, whereas the mudbrick wall evidence and ceramic typology support a Late Bronze date. He also points to earlier excavators such as Kathleen Kenyon and Garstang, plus the Late Bronze cemetery, as corroborating evidence that there was a town associated with the cemetery. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=1241" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:41</a> | What does he think of Richard Steiner's recent Merneptah-related exodus proposal? | He rejects it outright as chronologically and textually untenable. | He says the proposal cannot make Merneptah both the exodus pharaoh and the pharaoh encountered by Israel in Canaan. Since the Merneptah stele belongs to Merneptah's fifth year and Merneptah reigned only about ten years, the proposal would compress the plagues, exodus, wilderness period, conquest activity, and encounter in Canaan into an impossible sequence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=1537" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:37</a> | Is Christ's resurrected body physically somewhere in heaven, or in some unseen dimension? | He says heaven appears able to accommodate embodied humans, pointing to Enoch and Elijah as examples of bodily presence there. | He says the Bible gives examples of Enoch and Elijah being taken into heaven without dying, which suggests heaven can in some way accommodate embodied human beings. He avoids explaining the mechanism, but argues that heaven includes space and place, and physicality requires space and place, so Christ's embodied presence there is not ruled out. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=1839" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:39</a> | Are donations to the channel tax deductible, and is PayPal better than Super Chat? | He says PayPal is better for the channel because it loses less to fees and is treated as a gift, while the donations are generally not tax deductible. | He distinguishes PayPal gifts from Super Chats, saying PayPal takes less and is not treated as a paid exchange for service, while YouTube and taxes take substantial portions of Super Chat revenue. On deductibility, he says the channel is not set up as a charity and, even if it were Canadian charity status, most foreign donors would likely not get a useful deduction; he also cautions that he is not giving tax advice. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=2082" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:42</a> | Should Christians claim tithes on their taxes when the law allows it? | Yes; he says believers should pay what they owe without cheating, but they should not overpay by refusing lawful deductions. | He frames the question as giving Caesar what belongs to Caesar, not more than Caesar is legally owed. If a government allows a legal deduction for charitable or tithe giving, he says claiming it is proper stewardship because the money can go to further charity rather than unnecessary overpayment to the government. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=2260" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:40</a> | What is the context of Leiden 348, and why is it not talked about more? | He says the text in question is not the sensational "Habiru built Ramesses" proof some claim, and he starts by identifying it more carefully before drawing conclusions. | After looking it up, he identifies Leiden 348 with Ipuwer and says it is a 12th Dynasty text, not a 19th Dynasty record of Habiru building Ramesses. He says translations are widely available in anthologies, preferring Simpson over Pritchard or Lichtheim, and rejects the claim that it describes Ramesside construction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=2892" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:12</a> | Is there legitimacy to the dual-causality principle in the Old Testament and ancient Near Eastern literature? | He says he is not really qualified to answer the philosophical side and treats his response as limited rather than definitive. | He defines dual causality as both human decisions and divine sovereignty bearing on events, then says he has no problem with the basic idea and compares it to primary and secondary causes. He notes that the topic is debated in philosophy, where he is not current, but says multiple perspectives on one event, including divine and human perspectives, are plausible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=3396" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">56:36</a> | Will God publicly reveal people's earthly deeds like a movie after death? | Not like a movie, in his view; he says there will be an accounting that shows God's judgments are just and that rewards and punishments differ. | He rejects the idea of a literal movie-like replay but affirms that the books will be opened and deeds will be accounted for. The purpose, in his view, is not entertainment or mere shame but to show that God's judgments are just and to explain differing rewards and punishments. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=3799" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:03:19</a> | What does it mean to "work out your salvation" in Philippians 2:12-13 if believers are already saved? | He says the passage is about living out salvation's implications with proper nuance, not denying that believers already belong to Christ. | He reads Philippians as addressed to believers who have already obeyed and are not merely performing in Paul's presence. He says the term can carry the sense of preservation, so the exhortation means to keep living faithfully, stay on the right path, and continue storing treasure in heaven rather than earn initial salvation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=4427" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:47</a> | Are the achievements of Cain's line in Genesis 4:17-24 evil? | No; he says the achievements themselves are not evil, though some are retrojections and Cain's line is contrasted with Seth's line. | He says the listed achievements are not inherently evil, but some details, such as bronze and iron working before the flood, are retrojections from the time Genesis was put to pen. He also reads the passage as contrasting Cain's city-building line with Seth's pastoral line, a contrast that fits Israel's move from Egypt toward pastoral life in the land. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=4859" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:59</a> | How accurate are the reconstructed rules of Senet? | He says they are not accurate at all because nobody actually knows how Senet was played. | He gives a blunt answer: the reconstructed rules are essentially made up because the actual rules are not known. He notes that people even hold Senet tournaments today, but he still treats those rules as modern reconstructions rather than historically secure play. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=4911" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:51</a> | Do modern nations have angelic princes like Daniel 10:13 describes? | Yes; he identifies them with spiritual powers that influence nations and governments rather than directly possessing rulers. | He answers yes and connects Daniel's prince of Persia with powers described in passages such as Ephesians. In his explanation, these beings influence governments and nations subtly, by planting ideas and shaping rulers, rather than by directly possessing political leaders. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=5020" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:23:40</a> | If all power and authority have been given to Christ, why are these powers still in charge? | He says God permits evil for now, comparing it to the wheat and tares, and that Christ defeats these powers gradually. | He says Christ could remove the spiritual powers immediately, but God permits evil for now for reasons like the parable of the wheat and tares, where uprooting the tares too soon would also harm the wheat. He describes Christ's kingdom as advancing gradually through Christians, taking territory without destroying what should be preserved. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=5174" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:26:14</a> | Do these spiritual beings have names and individual wills over the nations they influence? | To a greater or lesser extent, yes; he says spirit beings have names and wills, though they control countries only partially. | He says spirit beings have names and wills just as humans do, but their control over countries is partial and variable. He distinguishes powers over governments from lower-ranking cosmic powers over movements, organizations, and social or religious trends. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=5364" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:24</a> | How does one defeat or conquer such spiritual powers? | He says people cannot directly defeat them; they may destroy movements or nations they influence, but the powers move on. | He says humans cannot really touch or defeat these higher spiritual powers directly. At most, people can destroy the movements or nations those beings work through, while the beings themselves move on; he adds that beings like Michael can fight them, but that is not ordinary human action. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=5621" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:41</a> | Does God speak to people today like the prophets of old, or is that only a figure of speech? | He says God can speak today, but modern claims should be treated carefully and do not carry the same weight as biblical prophecy. | He says nothing prevents God from speaking today, because God is limited only by the limits he sets for himself. However, he distinguishes modern claims from biblical prophecy, saying biblical prophets were tied to God's throne-room council in a way modern personal words or gifts of prophecy generally are not. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=5881" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:01</a> | How can someone legitimately confirm whether a person is a prophet today? | Compare the claim to Scripture; he says God's word does not contradict itself, so claimed revelation must be judged against prior revelation. | He says the church has always judged new claims by comparing them to what God has already revealed. He gives examples of Old Testament writings being compared to Torah, the New Testament being compared to the Old Testament, and later claims such as Mormon scriptures being rejected when they fail that test. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=6105" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:45</a> | How did today's mainline Protestants get hijacked? | He says theological and cultural factors contributed, especially approaches that let other authorities override Scripture. | He points especially to a version of prima scriptura where the Bible is consulted first but can then be modified by other authorities or cultural concerns. In his account, that made mainline Protestantism vulnerable to the zeitgeist because other preferred ideas could effectively override Scripture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=6251" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:44:11</a> | Is Ezekiel seeing the literal form of the cherubim? | No; he says Ezekiel is perceiving something real, but his mind and cultural framework supply imagery for spirit beings that are not literally bodies with wings and faces. | He says cherubim are spirit beings with essence and form, but not literal physical bodies with wings, faces, and animal parts. Ezekiel is perceiving something real, while his mind, shaped by cultural imagery and Mesopotamian influence, fills in comprehensible visionary imagery for what the spiritual form suggests. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=6576" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:36</a> | Do ghosts tend to haunt places where witchcraft or occult practice has happened? | No; he says hauntings are not limited to occult sites and could reportedly occur in places with no such connection. | He denies that hauntings are limited to places associated with witchcraft or the occult. As an example, he mentions reports of hauntings at the Victoria Museum at the University of Liverpool, while saying he knows of no witchcraft connection there and did not personally see anything when he visited. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=6641" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:50:41</a> | Can ghosts or spirits be caught on videotape or another recording device? | Generally not; he says ghosts do not interact with the electromagnetic spectrum in a way a camera or recorder could capture. | He says he is highly skeptical of video or audio recordings that purport to capture ghosts. Poltergeist effects such as objects moving could be recorded because the objects are physical, but ghosts themselves are perceived by the human mind and do not interact with the electromagnetic spectrum in the way recording devices require. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=6827" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:47</a> | When were angels created? | He says the answer depends on whether "angel" means any good spirit or specifically a messenger, and that "when were spirit beings created?" is the clearer question. | He separates the broad popular use of angel for good spirits from the narrower meaning of messenger. Reframing the question as when spirit beings were created, he says chaos monsters came first with the material universe, followed by primordial deities, watchers, and then other angelic beings such as cherubim, seraphim, and ophanim. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=7060" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:57:40</a> | Where can someone read more about primordial god deities? | He says no one has really written the needed monograph, and the only source he can point to at length is his own fiction treatment. | He says the subject badly needs a monograph and that, to his knowledge, no one has written one. He mentions using primordial deities in his fiction because the mythological material is rich but underdeveloped, and says scholars such as Kitchen were not really focused on angels, demons, or mythology of that kind. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=7214" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:00:14</a> | In Exodus 21:22-25, to whom is the death penalty relevant: the mother, the child, or both? | He reads the initial case as miscarriage without further injury, which brings a fine; if the woman is also injured or killed, lex talionis applies, including life for life. | He reads verse 22 as a case where the child is lost but the woman has no further injury, so compensation is a fine set through husband and judges. If the woman is injured beyond the miscarriage, then lex talionis applies proportionally: tooth for tooth, eye for eye, and life for life if she dies. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=7567" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:06:07</a> | Is Exodus 21:22 about miscarriage or premature birth? | He says it is miscarriage, though the miscarried child could be alive or dead in the scenario. | He says the passage is about miscarriage rather than ordinary premature birth, because punishment enters where there is injury or loss. He notes that a live child would not normally create grounds for compensation, and he distinguishes the Hebrew verb for "comes out" from the usual verb for giving birth. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=7802" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:02</a> | Why did the risen Christ not appear to enemies such as Pilate, since that might have helped Christianity grow? | He says appearing to enemies would not guarantee belief; people can reject even strong evidence, so Christ appeared first to the faithful. | He argues that appearing to enemies would not necessarily produce faith, since people can dismiss even direct supernatural evidence. He invokes the Lazarus parable's logic that those who reject Moses and the prophets will not be persuaded simply by an extraordinary message, so Christ appearing to the faithful first makes sense. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=8021" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:13:41</a> | Is Arioch of Ellasar in Genesis 14 Rim-Sin of Larsa? | Probably not; he says Rim-Sin's dates do not fit the likely chronology, though another local king could still be possible. | He dates the Genesis 14 campaign by associating Amraphel with Hammurabi and places the campaign around Hammurabi's fortieth year. Since Rim-Sin of Larsa had already been conquered by Hammurabi and likely died before that campaign, he says Rim-Sin does not fit, though another local king named Arioch might. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=8347" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:19:07</a> | How should one respond to someone who says he hears God and does not need or trust the Bible? | He calls that a formula for error and heresy, saying any claimed spiritual revelation must be checked against Scripture. | He says the claim that someone is closer to the source than Scripture is a formula for error and heresy, especially from a pastor whose job is to preach God's word. Any claimed spiritual revelation must be tested against Scripture, and he says people who abandon that standard tend to drift quickly into strange beliefs. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=8616" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:23:36</a> | Would he consider doing reaction videos to real paranormal-story podcast channels if sponsored? | Yes, if properly sponsored; he says such projects would need to be arranged through Discord and meet his consulting-rate threshold. | He says he is open to sponsored reaction projects, noting that previous special projects have been sponsored. The process would be to contact him on Discord, describe the request, and make sure the sponsorship matches his normal consulting rate rather than a token amount. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=8930" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:28:50</a> | What kinds of sins do people commit unknowingly or without realizing they are sins? | He says it depends on upbringing, but gives examples such as sexual sin, "white lies," gossip, slander, and other normalized behavior. | He says ignorance often depends on upbringing and poor instruction, giving the example of churchgoing people who had never been told that sex outside marriage was sin. He also highlights white lies, gossip, slander, social media behavior, and rationalized theft as sins people may normalize without recognizing them as sinful. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=9235" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:33:55</a> | What are his thoughts on Winger's Bethel revelations? | He sides with Winger's criticism and describes Bethel-related practices as false prophecy, bad translation work, and marketing-like spirituality. | He says he normally sides with Winger when Winger criticizes Bethel, which he describes as tied to counterfeit-revival practices. He criticizes false prophecies, attempts to teach prophecy, the Passion Translation, healing-class style spirituality, and what he sees as marketing around dubious spiritual practices. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=9720" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:42:00</a> | Do Job's complaints count as prayer since he is basically talking to God? | Mostly no; he treats many of them as poetic laments, though Job's direct speech to God near the end could count as prayer. | He says Job is not always talking directly to God, and much of the book is compressed poetic lament rather than prayer. Near the end, when Job responds directly to God's self-revelation, that speech could count as prayer, but the earlier complaints are better classified as laments. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=9822" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:43:42</a> | What would count as a prayer? | Talking to God and making appeals to God. | He defines prayer as talking to God and making appeals to him. By that definition, simply complaining or venting is not necessarily prayer unless the speech is actually directed to God as an appeal. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=9847" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:44:07</a> | Did the Egyptians record the seven-year famine from the Bible? | No; he says famines were common enough in Egypt that they were not consistently recorded, making identification difficult. | He says famines happened frequently in Egypt because agriculture depended on the Nile's height, with high Nile meaning abundance and low Nile meaning starvation. Since famines occurred several times a century and were only occasionally recorded, even a recorded famine would be hard to identify securely with the biblical seven-year famine. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=9951" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:45:51</a> | Did Job really happen, or is it just a story? | He says it is probably both: a real Job and historical core presented through mytho-history and poetic dramatization. | He thinks there was probably a historical Job, likely an Aramean in Syria around the post-Mitanni breakup, with details such as Chaldeans fitting an ancient historical setting. At the same time, he says the book turns Job's suffering into poetic and divine drama, so it is mytho-history rather than a bare transcript of events. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=10123" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:48:43</a> | What were the reasons behind Akhenaten's monotheism, if he was a monotheist? | He says Akhenaten was not a monotheist but a pantheistic monolatrist, driven partly by hostility toward Egypt's priesthoods. | He says Akhenaten was not a monotheist because the Aten theology treats everything as Aten and other gods as manifestations of Aten, which makes it pantheistic rather than transcendent monotheism. He still calls Akhenaten monolatrous because he worshiped one deity, and says his move was driven partly by hostility toward the Theban priesthood and other Egyptian priesthoods. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=10634" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:57:14</a> | Did God answer Cain about whether he was his brother's keeper? | No; he says God exposes Cain's misdirection instead, because Cain is not merely Abel's keeper but Abel's killer. | He says God does not answer Cain's evasive question because the answer is already obvious and Cain is trying to divert attention from his guilt. Instead, God exposes the truth by saying Abel's blood cries from the ground: Cain is not merely failing as Abel's keeper, he is Abel's killer. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=10785" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:59:45</a> | Did ancient Israelites view Satan the same way later Christianity did? | No; he says the Old Testament often uses "satan" as a job title for an adversary, while later Christianity treats Satan more as a proper name. | He says in the Old Testament "satan" means adversary and can describe a role rather than an evil being, even applying to the angel of the Lord opposing Balaam. In later Christian usage it becomes more of a proper name identified with the serpent, the fallen cherub, and the old dragon, so context matters. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=11010" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:03:30</a> | How can a man control inappropriate dreams or night emissions while sleeping in order to be right with God? | He says they cannot be controlled, are part of normal male biology, and carry no moral guilt because there is no premeditation or intent. | He says these episodes occur at a deep level of sleep and are biologically built into male physiology, so they are not something a person can control. Because there is no premeditation, intent, or controllable lust involved, he says there is no moral guilt and the person remains right with God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=11415" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:10:15</a> | What does he think about John Walton's view that the Mosaic law is wisdom literature? | He calls it a gross oversimplification and says he has reservations about Walton's later work. | He says he appreciated Walton's early solo work but thinks the quality declined in later work, especially collaborative work with Harvey Walton and other students. He criticizes Walton's later views on demons and Daniel, and treats the claim that Mosaic law is wisdom literature as an oversimplification. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=11655" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:14:15</a> | Could older scholars drift in their views because they lose inhibitions or feel pressure to produce novelty? | He says people often become more of what they already were, and that prior compromise can become greater compromise with age. | He does not say older scholars necessarily become unhinged; rather, he says age often reveals or amplifies earlier inclinations as people lose the energy to maintain masks. Applied to academic compromise, he suggests that people who learned to avoid risk or please the academy when young may become even more compromising later. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/Qs-d4ght8aU?t=11936" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:18:56</a> | What ethnicity or country would Jesus have considered himself? | He says Jesus would have considered himself Judean, a Jew from the tribe of Judah and line of David. | He answers that Jesus would have considered himself Judean: a Jew from Judah and from the line of David. He adds that Israelites in that region would have understood themselves in those terms, rather than by a modern national category. |
